title: REST API Design Principles
learningObjectives: Model resources, collections, and sub-resources with URLs that stay stable as the domain grows
Apply correct HTTP methods and status codes so caches, gateways, and client retries behave predictably
Design idempotent POST flows (Idempotency-Key), pagination (offset vs keyset), and error bodies (e.g. Problem Details)
Compare API versioning strategies and non-breaking evolution tactics used in real microservice fleets
Relate REST semantics to Java/Spring patterns: ResponseEntity, validation, and structured errors
Explain one concrete production failure tied to bad API semantics and how you would fix it

sections.title: Why REST API Design Principles matters
sections.content:
REST API design is where vague product requirements become a **contract** that mobile apps, BFFs, partners, and internal services depend on for years. Interviewers are rarely testing whether you memorized every line of RFC 9110 — they are testing whether you can **prevent the failure modes that show up in post-mortems**.

Typical incidents: **double charges** because `POST /payments` was retried without idempotency; **broken caches** because the API returned `200 OK` with an error payload; **retry storms** after ambiguous `5xx` without `Retry-After`; **pagination** that works on page 1 but times out at offset 50,000; **silent consumer breakage** when response shapes change without a version or contract tests.

When you explain REST well, you tie **resource layout and HTTP semantics** to **operational behavior**: what proxies may cache, what clients may safely retry, and how errors map to remediation (fix the payload vs refresh auth vs exponential backoff). That is the gap between a textbook answer and one that sounds like you have **shipped** APIs.

A strong Day 49 answer usually does four things: (1) state what **problem boundary** the API solves for the domain, (2) justify **URLs + verbs + status codes** for that boundary, (3) name **one realistic incident** caused by a bad contract choice, (4) describe **mitigation** — idempotency store, RFC 7807-style errors, ETags, keyset pagination, explicit versioning, OpenAPI + consumer tests in CI.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - REST API Design Principles
sections.content:
### Day 49 — REST API design (deep dive)

This day is about **correct HTTP semantics**, **resource modeling**, and **contracts** that survive retries, caches, multiple teams, and years of evolution — not about memorizing buzzwords.

---

### 1. What REST actually is
Roy Fielding's REST couples **resources**, **representations**, and a **uniform interface** with constraints: **client–server**, **stateless**, **cacheable**, **layered system**, and (optionally) **code on demand**. In practice most internal JSON APIs are **not** full hypermedia machines; they aim for **clear resources + correct verbs/status codes + a documented contract** (often **OpenAPI**). The **Richardson Maturity Model** is a useful interview frame: Level 0 (one POST tunnel), Level 1 (many resources), Level 2 (verbs + status codes), Level 3 (HATEOAS). Mature teams often stop at **Level 2 + good docs**, using links only where discoverability pays off (public/partner APIs).

---

### 2. Resource modeling — the skill interviewers probe
- **Collections** → plural nouns: `GET /orders`, `POST /orders`.
- **Single resource** → stable opaque id in path: `GET /orders/{orderId}` (prefer UUID/ULID in **public** APIs to avoid enumeration and coupling to DB sequences).
- **Sub-resources** express **containment**: e.g. `/customers/{id}/payment-methods` when lifecycle is owned by the parent.
- **Default style** is noun-oriented URLs; **verb paths** like `/createOrder` are usually a smell. **Domain actions** that are not CRUD (`POST /subscriptions/{id}/cancel`) are acceptable as **documented exceptions**, not the default pattern.
- **Actions vs resources:** if everything becomes `POST /doSomething`, you lose **cacheability** and **uniform error** semantics — you have rebuilt RPC over HTTP.

---

### 3. HTTP methods — what proxies and clients assume
| Method   | Safe | Idempotent (when done right) | Typical use |
|----------|------|------------------------------|-------------|
| GET/HEAD | Yes  | Yes                          | Read |
| OPTIONS  | Yes  | Yes                          | CORS/preflight, discovery |
| PUT      | No   | Yes                          | Replace representation at a known URI |
| DELETE   | No   | Yes                          | Remove; second delete → often 404 (still idempotent in effect) |
| POST     | No   | **No** unless you add idempotency | Create, commands, multi-step workflows |
| PATCH    | No   | **Not guaranteed**            | Partial update — you must define semantics (merge vs JSON Patch) |

**Interview angle:** explain why **GET must not change server state** (caches, prefetchers, monitoring pings). Explain why **PUT** repeats should converge to the **same** resource state, and why naive **POST** retries can duplicate **side effects**.

---

### 4. Status codes — decision guide (what to say in interviews)
- **200** — success with body; **204** — success, no body (common after DELETE).
- **201 Created** — resource created; send **`Location`** and usually a representation; clients and tools depend on this.
- **400** — malformed syntax/types; **422** (common convention) — JSON valid but **business/validation** rules failed.
- **401** — **not authenticated**; **403** — authenticated but **not allowed** — confusing these breaks security UX and logs.
- **404** — not found vs **410 Gone** — deliberately removed.
- **409 Conflict** — state conflict (duplicate unique key, illegal transition).
- **412 Precondition Failed** — failed **`If-Match`** / optimistic concurrency.
- **429** — rate limited; add **`Retry-After`** when possible.
- **503** — overload/maintenance; **`Retry-After`** enables cooperative backoff.

**Anti-pattern:** `200 OK` with `{ "error": ... }` — intermediaries and generic HTTP clients cannot classify failure; retries break.

Prefer a **stable machine-readable error** shape: **RFC 7807 Problem Details** (`type`, `title`, `status`, `detail`, `instance`) plus **correlation/trace id** for support.

---

### 5. Idempotency keys and safe retries
Networks and gateways **retry**. For **POST** that moves money, inventory, or tickets, use **`Idempotency-Key`**: server stores `(key → outcome fingerprint)` for a TTL and **replays the same response** without re-running side effects. Document **scope** (per user? per merchant?) and **TTL**.

---

### 6. Pagination, sorting, filtering
**Offset + limit** is easy but **expensive** for deep pages in SQL (`OFFSET` large) and **unstable** if rows move. **Keyset (cursor)** on a **stable sort** (e.g. `(created_at DESC, id DESC)`) scales better; expose an **opaque** `next_cursor`. Cap **`limit`** and document max result sets.

---

### 7. Versioning and evolution
Options: **URL** (`/v1/...`), **header** (`Accept` vendor type), or **hostname**. Pick **one org standard**. Prefer **additive** changes (new optional fields). Breaking renames/removals → **new version**, **deprecation window**, **Sunset** policy, **consumer contract tests** in CI.

---

### 8. Caching and conditional requests
Correct **`Cache-Control`** on **GET** controls CDN/browser behavior. **ETag** + **`If-None-Match`** (304) saves bandwidth for polling. Mutations should invalidate or document cache interaction for clients you control.

---

### 9. Java / Spring (practical mapping)
**`@RestController`**, **`ResponseEntity`** for status + headers, **`@Valid`** for input validation with consistent **400/422**, **Spring 6 `ProblemDetail`** for structured errors, **`Page` DTOs** for pagination — tie these to the HTTP semantics above, not as isolated annotations.

---

### 10. Story you can tell in 60 seconds
**Symptom:** Checkout **timeouts** led partners to **retry**; inventory **oversold**. **Root cause:** **`POST /orders`** not idempotent + ambiguous **504** handling. **Mitigation:** **`Idempotency-Key`** + dedup store, explicit **409** when business rules block, documented **retry** policy, **idempotent** inventory hold or saga — shows you connect **API design** to **data integrity**.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough — Resource Naming & HTTP Method Properties
sections.output:
=== REST Resource URL Patterns ===
Collection      GET    https://api.example.com/orders
Single item     GET    https://api.example.com/orders/ord-789
Sub-resource    GET    https://api.example.com/orders/ord-789/items
Create          POST   https://api.example.com/orders
Replace full    PUT    https://api.example.com/orders/ord-789
Partial update  PATCH  https://api.example.com/orders/ord-789
Delete          DELETE https://api.example.com/orders/ord-789
Domain action   POST   https://api.example.com/orders/ord-789/cancel

=== HTTP Method Properties ===
Method    Safe    Idempotent  Notes
GET       Yes     Yes         Cacheable; never change state
HEAD      Yes     Yes         Same as GET, no response body
OPTIONS   Yes     Yes         CORS preflight / capability probe
PUT       No      Yes         Replaces full resource; safe to retry
DELETE    No      Yes         Second call may get 404, effect same
POST      No      No*         Creates/commands; needs Idempotency-Key
PATCH     No      No*         Partial; define merge semantics explicitly

* POST/PATCH are idempotent ONLY when you add Idempotency-Key design

=== URL Design: Good vs Smell ===
GOOD  POST /orders              (noun, collection)
SMELL POST /orders/create       (verb in URL = RPC smell)
GOOD  POST /orders/ord-789/cancel  (documented domain action)
SMELL GET  /cancelOrder?id=789  (GET must never mutate state)
GOOD  GET  /users/usr-42/addresses  (sub-resource containment)
SMELL GET  /getUserAddresses?userId=42  (query param as path)
sections.description: Covers REST resource URL patterns (collections, single items, sub-resources, domain actions) and the HTTP method safety/idempotency matrix that drives retry and caching behavior.

sections.title: Intermediate — Idempotency-Key Pattern for POST /orders
sections.output:
=== Idempotency-Key Pattern: POST /orders ===

Call 1 — first request from client:
  [NEW]   Order created, key cached. orderId=ord-1001
  Status   : 201
  Body     : {"orderId":"ord-1001","status":"PENDING","items":[PROD-1, PROD-2]}
  Location: /orders/ord-1001

Call 2 — client retries with SAME Idempotency-Key:
  [DEDUP] Replaying cached response. key=req-c8f3a1b2
  Status   : 201
  Body     : {"orderId":"ord-1001","status":"PENDING","items":[PROD-1, PROD-2]}
  Location: /orders/ord-1001

Call 3 — new key = new order:
  [NEW]   Order created, key cached. orderId=ord-1002
  Status   : 201
  Body     : {"orderId":"ord-1002","status":"PENDING","items":[PROD-5]}
  Location: /orders/ord-1002

Call 4 — validation failure (empty items):
  Status   : 422
  Body     : {"type":"validation-error","detail":"items required"}

=== Key Design Rules ===
Rule 1: Same key + same payload  -> replay cached response (no double charge)
Rule 2: Same key + DIFF payload  -> 409 Conflict (key misuse)
Rule 3: Key scope               -> per (userId, key) in Redis
Rule 4: TTL                     -> 24 hours is a common default
Rule 5: Key generation          -> UUID v4 generated by client, not server
sections.description: Full idempotency-key deduplication pattern: first call creates and caches, retry replays without re-running side effects, new key creates a new resource, validation failure returns 422. Mirrors production Redis-backed pattern.

sections.title: Advanced — RFC 7807 Problem Details + Keyset Pagination + Status Reference
sections.output:
=== Keyset Pagination ===
Why keyset? OFFSET 50000 requires DB to skip 50000 rows — slow & unstable
Keyset: WHERE id < lastSeenId ORDER BY id DESC — uses index, stable

Page 1 orders : [ord-900, ord-899, ord-898, ord-897, ord-896]
nextCursor    : cur_896  <-- opaque, client passes back as-is
hasMore       : true

Page 2 orders : [ord-895, ord-894, ord-893, ord-892, ord-891]
nextCursor    : cur_891
hasMore       : true

=== RFC 7807 Problem Details ===
{"type":"insufficient-stock","title":"Insufficient Stock","status":422,"detail":"SKU PROD-42: requested 10, available 3","instance":"/orders/ord-789","traceId":"trace-abc123"}
{"type":"idempotency-conflict","title":"Idempotency Key Conflict","status":409,"detail":"Key 'req-A1B2' already used with a different request body","instance":"/orders","traceId":"trace-def456"}
{"type":"rate-limited","title":"Too Many Requests","status":429,"detail":"Limit 100 req/min exceeded. Retry-After: 47 seconds","instance":"/orders","traceId":"trace-ghi789"}

=== Status Code Decision Reference ===
201 Created               Resource created — always include Location header
204 No Content            Success, no body (DELETE, some PUT)
304 Not Modified          ETag matched — client uses its cached copy
400 Bad Request           Malformed JSON, wrong types
401 Unauthorized          No/invalid auth — add WWW-Authenticate header
403 Forbidden             Valid auth, insufficient permission
404 Not Found             Resource absent (also used to hide 403)
409 Conflict              Duplicate key, illegal state transition
412 Precondition Failed   If-Match ETag mismatch (optimistic lock)
422 Unprocessable Entity  Valid JSON but business rules violated
429 Too Many Requests     Rate limited — add Retry-After header
503 Service Unavailable   Overloaded/maintenance — add Retry-After
sections.description: Combines three advanced REST API topics in one runnable file: keyset pagination vs offset (why it scales), RFC 7807 Problem Details error format used by Spring 6, and a complete HTTP status code decision reference.

sections.title: Request path — REST semantics in production
sections.description: How a REST request flows through gateway and service; where idempotency, status codes, and tracing matter.
sections.plantuml:
@startuml
title Day 49 — REST request path
actor Client
participant Gateway
participant "Order API" as API
database "Store" as DB

Client -> Gateway : POST /orders + Idempotency-Key + traceparent
Gateway -> API : auth, rate limit, route
API -> DB : dedup key? persist resource
API --> Gateway : 201 Location / 409 / ProblemDetail
Gateway --> Client : stable status + body

note right of API
  Correct 4xx/5xx + headers
  enable retries & caches
end note
@enduml

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using GET for state-changing operations — breaks caching, prefetch, and safe-retry assumptions. Monitoring pings and CDN crawlers may trigger unintended mutations.
Returning HTTP 200 with an error JSON body — intermediaries, APM tools, and generic clients cannot classify the call as failed. Alarms, dashboards, and retry logic all break silently.

Deep offset pagination on large tables without a keyset alternative — `OFFSET 50000` forces the database to scan and discard rows; latency grows linearly and the result set shifts as rows are inserted/deleted.
Treating 401 and 403 as interchangeable — 401 means 'provide credentials'; 403 means 'credentials rejected'. Confusing them breaks auth UX, security audits, and automated client retry decisions.
POST for payments or inventory without Idempotency-Key — duplicate side effects when clients or API gateways retry on timeout. One network hiccup becomes two charges.

Changing response shapes without versioning or consumer contract tests — removing a field, renaming a property, or changing a type silently breaks every consumer. CI never catches it because the producer's tests pass.
Using sequential integer IDs in public URLs — enables enumeration attacks (`/orders/1`, `/orders/2`, ...) and couples your API contract to DB auto-increment. Prefer UUID or ULID.

Ignoring `Retry-After` on 429 and 503 — clients that do not receive a retry hint will hammer the service with exponential backoff anyway, or worse, with fixed short intervals. Always signal when to retry.

sections.title: Exercise - Design POST /orders for an Unreliable Network
sections.problem:
Design **POST /orders** for a partner-facing API over **unreliable networks** (timeouts and retries are normal).

Requirements:
1. Specify which **HTTP status codes** you return for: created, validation error, auth failure, business conflict, and duplicate retry.
2. Describe how **`Idempotency-Key`** is validated and what you store server-side (TTL, scope).
3. Explain what the client receives on a **duplicate** POST with the same key after a successful first call.
4. Name **one trade-off** (e.g. dedup storage vs replay window).
5. Optional: map one response to **Spring** (`ResponseEntity`, `ProblemDetail`).

You may answer in structured prose and short Java/pseudocode in comments — the goal is API semantics, not a large codebase.
hints: Pair 201 + Location with create; use 409 or 422 consistently for your domain rules.
Document that retries must reuse the same Idempotency-Key for the same logical operation.
Mention correlation/trace id in error responses for operations.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is the REST architectural style? What are its constraints?
conceptual.answer: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an **architectural style** — not a protocol — defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 dissertation. Six constraints: **client–server** (UI separated from data storage), **stateless** (each request is self-contained, no server-side session between requests), **cacheable** (responses explicitly label themselves as cacheable or not), **uniform interface** (URI identifies resources; clients manipulate via representations; self-descriptive messages; HATEOAS optional), **layered system** (client cannot tell if it's talking to origin server or proxy), and optional **code on demand** (server may send executable code). In practice, 'RESTful' usually means: plural-noun URIs, correct HTTP verbs, proper status codes, and a documented OpenAPI contract. Most internal APIs stop at Richardson Level 2 (HTTP verbs + status codes) and skip full HATEOAS.

conceptual.question: What does idempotent mean? Which HTTP methods are idempotent and why?
conceptual.answer: **Idempotent** means: making the same request N times produces the same **server state** as making it once (the response may differ, but the effect converges). **GET, HEAD, OPTIONS** are idempotent **and** safe (no side effects). **PUT** is idempotent: it replaces the full resource at a known URI — send the same body twice, the resource ends up in the same state. **DELETE** is idempotent: after deletion the resource is gone; a second DELETE may return 404 but the effect (resource absent) is the same. **POST** is **not** idempotent by default: `POST /orders` twice creates two orders. **PATCH** is not automatically idempotent — the semantics depend on how you define merge (e.g., append-to-list vs set-field). POST and PATCH can be **designed** to be idempotent by adding `Idempotency-Key` deduplication. Idempotency matters for **retry safety**: load balancers and clients can safely retry an idempotent call on timeout.

conceptual.question: What is the Richardson Maturity Model? Where do most real-world APIs sit?
conceptual.answer: The **Richardson Maturity Model** describes four levels of REST adoption: **Level 0** — one URI, one HTTP method (POX/SOAP tunnel, e.g. `POST /api` for everything). **Level 1** — multiple URIs representing resources, but still using one method. **Level 2** — correct HTTP verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) and meaningful status codes per operation — this is where most production APIs live. **Level 3** — HATEOAS: responses include hypermedia links that tell clients what actions are available next (`_links` in HAL, `actions` in Siren). Full HATEOAS is common in **public APIs** and SDKs where discoverability matters, but most **internal microservice** APIs stop at Level 2 with a well-maintained OpenAPI document instead of runtime links.

conceptual.question: When do you return 400 vs 422? When 401 vs 403?
conceptual.answer: **400 Bad Request** — the request could not be parsed or has wrong types: malformed JSON, missing required header, numeric field passed a string. The problem is at the **protocol/syntax** level. **422 Unprocessable Entity** — the JSON parsed successfully but **business/domain rules** failed: stock insufficient, account suspended, duplicate email, invalid state transition. The server understood the request but cannot process it. (Some teams use 400 for both; the important thing is to pick one convention and apply it consistently.) **401 Unauthorized** — the caller is **not authenticated**: no token, expired token, invalid signature. The fix is to log in and get a valid credential. HTTP spec requires `WWW-Authenticate` header. **403 Forbidden** — the caller **is** authenticated but does **not have permission** for this resource/action. The fix is to request a higher privilege or contact an admin. Confusing 401 and 403 breaks security auditing, client retry logic, and UX error messages.

conceptual.question: What is the Idempotency-Key pattern? When and why do you use it?
conceptual.answer: **Idempotency-Key** is a client-generated unique ID (usually UUID v4) sent as a request header (e.g. `Idempotency-Key: a3f8c1d2-...`). The **server** stores `(key → response fingerprint)` with a TTL (typically 24 h in Redis using `SETNX + EXPIRE`). On a **duplicate request with the same key**, the server replays the cached response **without re-executing side effects** (no second DB write, no second charge, no second event). This makes **POST** safe to retry. Use it for any operation with **non-idempotent side effects**: payments, order creation, inventory holds, email sends. Key design decisions: (1) **scope** — key is per `(userId, key)` pair, not globally unique; (2) **TTL** — 24 h balances replay window vs Redis size; (3) **conflict handling** — same key + different payload → 409 Conflict, not a replay.

conceptual.question: Offset vs keyset (cursor) pagination — what are the trade-offs?
conceptual.answer: **Offset pagination** (`?page=5&size=20` → SQL `OFFSET 100 LIMIT 20`) is simple and allows random-access page jumps. Problems: (1) **Performance degrades** at high offsets — the DB must scan and discard all preceding rows; `OFFSET 50000` can be 100x slower than `OFFSET 0` on a large table. (2) **Unstable results** — if a row is inserted or deleted between page 1 and page 2 requests, items shift and you see duplicates or miss items. **Keyset (cursor) pagination** uses the last-seen sort key (`WHERE (created_at, id) < (:lastCreatedAt, :lastId) ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20`). (1) **Constant performance** regardless of how deep — the DB uses the sort index directly. (2) **Stable** — new inserts do not shift existing pages. Trade-off: keyset requires a **stable sort column with an index**, cannot jump to an arbitrary page, and you expose an opaque cursor (base-64 encoded sort key) instead of page numbers. Use keyset for **large datasets and feeds**; offset for small admin tables where page-jump UX matters.

conceptual.question: What is RFC 7807 Problem Details? Why is it better than a custom error shape?
conceptual.answer: **RFC 7807 Problem Details for HTTP APIs** defines a standard JSON error body with fields: `type` (URI identifying the problem class), `title` (human-readable short description), `status` (HTTP status code), `detail` (human-readable specific explanation), `instance` (URI identifying the specific occurrence). Example: `{"type":"https://api.example.com/problems/insufficient-stock","title":"Insufficient Stock","status":422,"detail":"SKU PROD-42: requested 10, available 3","instance":"/orders/ord-789"}`. Benefits: (1) **Machine-readable** `type` URI allows clients to switch on error class without string matching. (2) **Standard field names** across all services — one SDK handles all errors. (3) **Extendable** — you add `traceId`, `errors[]` as extension fields. (4) **Spring 6** ships `ProblemDetail` natively (`ResponseEntityExceptionHandler` emits it by default for `@Valid` failures). The alternative — a custom `{"error":"...","message":"..."}` — forces every consumer to write bespoke error parsing.

conceptual.question: How do you version a REST API? What are the trade-offs of URL vs header versioning?
conceptual.answer: Three main strategies: (1) **URL versioning** (`/v1/orders`, `/v2/orders`) — the version is visible in logs, browser history, and API gateways. Routing is trivial. The downside is that `/v1` and `/v2` are treated as different resources by HTTP caches (same ETag on `/v1/orders/1` and `/v2/orders/1` is unrelated). (2) **Accept header / content negotiation** (`Accept: application/vnd.example.orders+json;version=2`) — keeps URIs clean and stable; the resource is the same, only the representation changes. Harder to test in browsers and tools; requires disciplined gateway routing. (3) **Hostname** (`v2.api.example.com`) — clean separation, easy DNS routing, but heavyweight. **Best practice regardless of strategy**: (a) **pick one** standard org-wide, (b) prefer **additive non-breaking changes** (new optional fields) to avoid bumping versions at all, (c) set a **deprecation window** with `Sunset` response header, (d) use **consumer contract tests** (e.g. Pact) in CI so breaking changes are caught before deploy.

conceptual.question: How do conditional GET and ETags work? Why do they matter?
conceptual.answer: The server includes an **`ETag`** header (a hash of the resource representation, e.g. `ETag: "a1b2c3d4"`) in GET responses. The client caches the response along with the ETag. On the **next GET**, the client sends `If-None-Match: "a1b2c3d4"`. If the resource is **unchanged**, the server returns **304 Not Modified** with no body — saving bandwidth and server CPU for serialization. If **changed**, the server returns **200 OK** with the new body and a new ETag. For **writes**, `If-Match: "a1b2c3d4"` enables **optimistic locking**: the server applies the update only if the current ETag matches; if someone else has modified it meanwhile, the server returns **412 Precondition Failed** instead of silently overwriting (lost update problem). In Spring: `ResponseEntity.ok().eTag(etag).body(resource)` and `@RequestHeader("If-None-Match") String ifNoneMatch` in the controller.

conceptual.question: HATEOAS in REST — what is it, when is it actually useful in production?
conceptual.answer: **HATEOAS (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State)** means that API responses include links describing which **actions/transitions** are available next, rather than requiring clients to have prior knowledge of URLs. Example response for an order: `{"orderId":"ord-789","status":"PENDING","_links":{"self":"/orders/ord-789","cancel":"/orders/ord-789/cancel","pay":"/orders/ord-789/payment"}}`. Benefits: the server can **change URLs** without breaking clients (clients follow links, not hardcoded paths); API is **self-describing**. In practice: **public partner APIs and SDKs** (e.g. PayPal, Stripe) use HATEOAS to reduce coupling and documentation overhead. **Internal microservice** APIs usually skip it — teams maintain OpenAPI specs and know the URLs at build time; the operational complexity of runtime link generation is not worth it. The pragmatic answer in an interview: 'useful for public/exploratory APIs; most internal teams stop at Level 2 with good OpenAPI docs.'

conceptual.question: How does rate limiting work in REST APIs? What headers should you return?
conceptual.answer: Rate limiting caps how many requests a client can make in a time window to protect the server and enforce fair use. Common algorithms: **token bucket** (smooth bursts allowed), **leaky bucket** (strict rate), **sliding window log** (precise but memory-heavy), **fixed window counter** (simple, slight burst at window boundary). HTTP semantics: when a client exceeds the limit, return **429 Too Many Requests** with a `Retry-After` header indicating when to retry (absolute date or seconds). Good APIs also return proactive headers on every response: `X-RateLimit-Limit` (window cap), `X-RateLimit-Remaining` (requests left), `X-RateLimit-Reset` (epoch when counter resets). Without `Retry-After`, well-behaved clients use exponential backoff but ill-behaved ones hammer the service continuously. Rate limits should be **scoped** (per API key, per user, per IP) and **documented** in the OpenAPI spec.

conceptual.question: What is content negotiation? When does 406 Not Acceptable get returned?
conceptual.answer: **Content negotiation** is the mechanism by which client and server agree on the format of the response. The client sends an `Accept` header listing its preferred media types (e.g. `Accept: application/json, application/xml;q=0.8, */*;q=0.5`). The server chooses the best match and responds with `Content-Type` indicating what it actually sent. If the server cannot produce any of the client's requested formats, it returns **406 Not Acceptable**. Similarly, for request bodies, the client declares `Content-Type: application/json` and if the server cannot process that type it returns **415 Unsupported Media Type**. Content negotiation is also used for **API versioning via Accept headers**: `Accept: application/vnd.example.api+json;version=2`. In practice, 406 is rare because most REST APIs produce only `application/json`; but in public APIs with XML/CSV support it matters.

conceptual.question: What is JSON Patch vs JSON Merge Patch? How do you choose for PATCH endpoints?
conceptual.answer: Two RFC standards for partial updates: **JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396)** — send a JSON object where present fields overwrite, and fields set to `null` are deleted. Simple and readable. Limitation: you cannot distinguish 'set field to null' from 'do not touch this field' in many scenarios; also cannot address array elements individually. Content-Type: `application/merge-patch+json`. **JSON Patch (RFC 6902)** — send an array of operations (`add`, `remove`, `replace`, `move`, `copy`, `test`), each with a JSON Pointer path. Precise, supports atomic test-then-modify, and handles arrays correctly. More verbose. Content-Type: `application/json-patch+json`. **How to choose**: use **Merge Patch** for simple document-style resources where you update top-level fields and null means delete. Use **JSON Patch** when you need atomic conditional operations, array manipulation, or when you want to guarantee no data is accidentally erased. Always document which semantics you implement — PATCH without documented semantics is a common source of bugs.

conceptual.question: How do you design bulk/batch endpoints in REST?
conceptual.answer: Three common patterns: (1) **Batch request body** — `POST /orders/batch` with an array of create requests; server processes all and returns a **207 Multi-Status** response with per-item status. Good when items are independent. (2) **Async batch job** — `POST /batch-jobs` creates a job resource; client polls `GET /batch-jobs/{id}` for status. Good for large, long-running batches. Returns 202 Accepted immediately. (3) **Bulk import via file** — client uploads CSV/JSON file; server processes in background. Trade-offs of sync batch: (a) **all-or-nothing vs partial success** — define which; 207 signals partial; (b) **payload size limits** — cap batch size and document it; (c) **timeout risk** for large batches on sync endpoints. Idempotency at the item level: each item in a batch request should carry its own idempotency key or natural unique key so the server can deduplicate partial replays. Document the exact failure semantics — clients must know if a partial failure rolls everything back or commits successful items.

conceptual.question: What is the API deprecation and Sunset process?
conceptual.answer: Deprecation is the process of signaling to consumers that a version or endpoint will be removed so they have time to migrate. Best practices: (1) **Sunset header** (RFC 8594) — include `Sunset: Sat, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00 GMT` in responses for deprecated endpoints; clients and gateways can react programmatically. (2) **Deprecation header** — `Deprecation: true` or a timestamp marking when deprecation started. (3) **Documentation** — mark endpoint deprecated in OpenAPI spec with `deprecated: true`; add migration guide. (4) **Usage analytics** — monitor which API keys are still calling deprecated endpoints; reach out to consumers before the sunset date. (5) **Compatibility window** — at minimum 3–6 months for internal consumers, 6–12 months for external partners (check contracts). (6) **Consumer contract tests** — running provider contract tests for all consumers in CI catches breaking changes automatically before deploy. Skipping a proper deprecation process is a common source of production outages when a team removes an endpoint they thought was unused.

codeBased.question: Show how to return 201 Created with a Location header in Spring
Show how to return 204 No Content after a DELETE in Spring
Show RFC 7807 ProblemDetail for a validation error in Spring 6
Show a keyset-paginated GET /orders response DTO
Show ETag conditional GET implementation in Spring
Show an Idempotency-Key filter/interceptor sketch in Spring
Show how to implement header-based API versioning in Spring
Show a bulk operation endpoint returning 207 Multi-Status
Show mapping an exception to 409 Conflict with ProblemDetail
Show how to add rate-limit headers and return 429 in Spring

seniorScenario.question: Your team deployed a change that removed a field from GET /orders/{id}. Three partner integrations broke. How do you respond, and how do you prevent this in the future?
seniorScenario.answer: **Immediate response**: (1) Roll back the change or add the field back as a nullable deprecated field — minimise time-to-restore for partners. (2) Communicate to affected partners with a specific timeline. **Root cause**: no consumer contract tests; no deprecation window; team assumed the field was unused. **Prevention**: (1) **Consumer-driven contract tests** (Pact) — each consumer publishes a pact file stating what fields it reads; CI runs `pact-verify` on the provider repo and the build breaks if a field is removed. (2) **API linting** (Optic, Spectral) in CI — detect breaking changes (field removal, type change) between the current OpenAPI spec and the previous version before merge. (3) **Deprecation process** — mark field `deprecated: true` in OpenAPI, add `X-Deprecated-Field: fieldName` warning header, set a sunset date (minimum 3 months internal, 6 months external), monitor usage in access logs. **Cultural change**: define 'breaking change' in the team's definition of done.

seniorScenario.question: Your API serves 10 M requests/day. A partner reports timeouts on GET /orders. Logs show DB CPU spikes every few minutes. How do you diagnose and fix?
seniorScenario.answer: **Diagnosis steps**: (1) Check slow query log — likely deep-offset pagination (`OFFSET 50000+`) or a missing index on the filter column. (2) Check EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the slow query — look for sequential scans. (3) Check if pagination params are unbounded — `limit=10000` causing massive result sets. (4) Check if `GET /orders` is missing a `Cache-Control` header — partners may be polling every second instead of using ETags. **Fixes by root cause**: (a) **Deep offset** → migrate to keyset pagination on `(created_at DESC, id DESC)` with a covering index — eliminates the O(N) scan. (b) **Missing index** → add composite index on filter columns (`status, customer_id`). (c) **No caching** → add `Cache-Control: max-age=30` for list endpoints; add `ETag` on individual resources; partners polling unchanged data will get 304 with no DB hit. (d) **Unbounded limit** → cap at 100 with documented max; return 400 if exceeded. (e) **Thundering herd** → if cache expires simultaneously, use cache stampede protection (probabilistic early refresh or Redis lock).

seniorScenario.question: A finance team asks for POST /payments to be made safe to retry because their gateway retries on 504. How do you implement Idempotency-Key end-to-end?
seniorScenario.answer: **Protocol contract**: Document that `Idempotency-Key` (UUID v4) is required for POST /payments. Enforce it — return 400 if missing. **Server-side store**: Redis hash `SETNX idem:{userId}:{key} 'PENDING' EX 86400`. Transition: (1) On first call — check Redis, get miss, proceed, set key to 'PROCESSING', run payment, update key to serialized `{status, paymentId, amount}`. (2) On duplicate — get hit, return cached response. Use **Redis Lua script** to make check-and-set atomic (no TOCTOU race between two simultaneous retries with the same key). **Conflict detection**: if same key is used with a different payload (amount or recipient changes), return 409 Conflict — this signals the client made a programming error. **TTL considerations**: 24 h means if the client retries after 25 h, they get a miss and could create a duplicate — document this and require clients to use a new key after TTL. **Downstream idempotency**: the payment processor call must also be idempotent (most processors accept their own idempotency-key header). **Testing**: load-test with 3 concurrent retries of the same key; assert only one DB row created.

seniorScenario.question: Your public API has external partners on v1 and internal services on v2. How do you manage this multi-version deployment?
seniorScenario.answer: **Routing strategy**: API gateway routes `/v1/*` → v1 deployment, `/v2/*` → v2 deployment (or same service with versioned controllers). **CI/CD**: both versions have separate integration test suites. Pact consumer tests run for all registered consumers against both provider versions. **Non-breaking changes**: add new optional fields to v1 and v2 simultaneously — consumers ignore unknown fields if they follow Postel's law. **Breaking changes in v2**: release v2 endpoint, maintain v1 side-by-side. Set `Sunset` header on v1 responses: `Sunset: 2025-12-01T00:00:00Z`. Monitor which API keys are still hitting v1 — send email/ticket to partners 90, 60, 30 days before sunset. **Shared business logic**: extract into a service layer that both v1 and v2 controllers call — avoid duplicating domain logic. **Eventually**: after v1 sunset date, add 410 Gone to v1 endpoints instead of immediate 404 so partners get a clear message. **Anti-pattern to avoid**: never silently forward v1 requests to v2 logic if there is a contract difference — partners will see unexpected data.

seniorScenario.question: GraphQL is proposed as an alternative to your REST API for a mobile client that suffers over-fetching. How do you evaluate the trade-off?
seniorScenario.answer: **REST problems that GraphQL solves**: (1) **Over-fetching** — `GET /users/{id}` returns 50 fields; mobile only needs 3. GraphQL query specifies exactly which fields are needed. (2) **Under-fetching / N+1 requests** — mobile needs user + their last 3 orders + each order's items → 4 round trips in REST vs 1 GraphQL query. (3) **Rapid frontend iteration** — frontend team can evolve queries without waiting for new REST endpoints. **GraphQL problems REST solves**: (1) **Caching** — REST GET responses are cacheable by CDN and browser (by URL); GraphQL uses POST and is not cacheable at HTTP layer without persisted queries. (2) **HTTP semantics** — status codes, idempotency, and observable monitoring are well-understood in REST. GraphQL returns 200 with `{"errors":[...]}` even on failure. (3) **Authorization complexity** — field-level authorization in GraphQL is harder to audit than endpoint-level in REST. (4) **N+1 on server side** — GraphQL requires DataLoader patterns to avoid N+1 DB queries for nested resolvers. **Recommendation**: introduce a **BFF (Backend for Frontend)** pattern — REST microservices internally, a thin GraphQL or REST BFF layer that aggregates data for mobile with only the fields needed. This gives mobile over-fetching relief without rebuilding the whole API.

seniorScenario.question: How would you design rate limiting for a public API that must be fair across 10,000 API keys with different tiers?
seniorScenario.answer: **Algorithm choice**: **token bucket** per API key — allows controlled bursts (a key can use a small burst after a quiet period) while enforcing an average rate. Leaky bucket is stricter; sliding window log is most precise but memory-heavy at scale. **Tiered limits**: store tier config in Redis hash or a config service: `{"free":100,"pro":1000,"enterprise":10000}` req/min. API key → tier mapping in Redis or DB with a short TTL cache (5 min) in the gateway. **Implementation**: API gateway (Kong, Envoy, AWS API Gateway) typically handles this with a Redis-backed rate limit plugin — do not implement in the application service itself. **Headers**: return `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`, `Retry-After` on 429 — cooperative clients back off. **Distributed correctness**: with multiple gateway replicas, use Redis with atomic Lua script (`INCR` + `EXPIRE`) or Redis Cell module. A small over-counting error is acceptable (eventual consistency); blocking undercounting is not. **DDoS vs legitimate rate limiting**: separate concerns — rate limiting protects fair use; DDoS mitigation (block entire IP ranges, challenge pages) is at the WAF/CDN layer before the gateway.

wrongAnswers: REST requires using JSON exclusively — REST is format-agnostic; XML, CSV, protobuf are all valid representations.
POST is always idempotent — POST is NOT idempotent by default; without Idempotency-Key, two POST /orders create two orders.

HTTP status codes are optional hints that clients can ignore — status codes are how HTTP infrastructure (proxies, caches, monitors) classifies responses; ignoring them breaks retries, alarms, and caching.
API versioning should change every sprint — versioning is for breaking changes; additive changes (new optional fields) must not bump the version.
HATEOAS means every response must include HTML links only — HATEOAS is about hypermedia controls in any format (HAL JSON, Siren); most REST APIs skip it entirely.
204 responses must include a JSON body — 204 No Content explicitly means no body; sending one violates the spec and will confuse HTTP clients.
401 and 403 mean the same thing — 401 = not authenticated (provide credentials); 403 = authenticated but not authorised (different fix path).

DELETE is not idempotent because the second call returns 404 — idempotency is about the server STATE, not the response code; after deletion the state is the same regardless of how many times you call it.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|---|---|---|
| URLs | Plural nouns, stable opaque IDs, sub-resources for containment | Collections vs items vs sub-resources; no verbs except domain actions |
| POST vs PUT | POST creates or triggers commands; PUT replaces full resource at known URI | POST not idempotent unless you design it; PUT safe to retry |
| Status codes | Never 200 for logical errors; body must match status | Use 4xx/5xx; 422 for business rules, 409 for conflicts |
| 401 vs 403 | 401 = not logged in; 403 = logged in, not allowed | Say both precisely — confused in 40% of codebases |
| Idempotency-Key | UUID client-generated, Redis store keyed per (userId, key), 24 h TTL | Same key replays response; diff payload + same key = 409 |
| Pagination | Prefer keyset on large tables; offset breaks at OFFSET 50000+ | `WHERE id < lastId ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT n` uses index |
| Errors | RFC 7807: type, title, status, detail, instance + traceId | Spring 6 ships ProblemDetail natively |
| Versioning | One org standard (URL or header); additive changes first | Breaking change = new version + deprecation window + Sunset header |
| Caching | GET + Cache-Control + ETag + If-None-Match | 304 Not Modified saves bandwidth; If-Match enables optimistic lock |
| HATEOAS | Useful for public/partner APIs; most internal APIs skip it | OpenAPI docs + Level 2 is the pragmatic sweet spot |
